feature.name: Email Verification Protocol
feature.summary: Helps users create, access and recover accounts by providing cryptographic proof of email ownership seamlessly rather than email OTPs/magic links.

intake.finding: Well-formed intake. ChromeStatus entry created 2025-09-05 (goto@google.com) with motivation, explainer, initial public proposal (WICG/proposals#239), bug/new-crbug links, and a clear, recurring problem: email verification today is insecure (phishing-prone magic links) and inefficient (delivery latency, spam folders, app-switching), spanning account creation, recovery, sign-in and 2FA. Target constituencies are explicit and multi-party (browsers, email providers, websites, users). Browser/platform intervention is plausibly the right layer. Component Blink>Identity; enterprise impact Low.
intake.limitations: No documented end-user research behind the intake; problem quantification (95% of top-50 sites use email auth; 73% block account creation on verification) is author-collected, not independently sourced.

incubation.finding: Incubated in WICG with a detailed public explainer including goals, non-goals, extensive alternatives (imperative vs declarative API; OTP/IMAP vs EVT; relationships to WebAuthn/FedCM/Digital Credentials API) and security/privacy considerations. However Mozilla filed a deferral (#1316) stating the explainer lacked sufficient detail on the problem statement and trade-offs vs OID/Passkeys, and TAG returned an ambivalent early review with a substantive Mozilla (Martin Thomson) critique on venue and design complexity. The explainer was later expanded.
incubation.limitations: No published developer-research ledger, surveys, or representative interviews; demand evidence is anecdotal.

prototype.finding: Implementation behind flag #email-verification-protocol (finch EmailVerificationProtocol), requires_embedder_support true, with a demo page and real Chromium implementation; Intent to Prototype filed 2025-09-06. But: not tested by web-platform-tests; debuggability marked still being developed; demo is a single self-authored page requiring Chrome Canary + flag + logged-in email domain; desktop-only initially.
prototype.limitations: No published friction logs, integration matrix, or task-based (non-unit) sample testing results from the prototype.

developerTrials.finding: A developer trial was run with a few partners who are expected to join the Origin Trial (basis for ChromeStatus Web developers: Positive), with DevTrial instructions in HOWTO.md. But no cohort roster, recruitment, methodology, task-completion, friction findings, or limitations were published; partners are unnamed, so the Positive signal is not independently auditable.
developerTrials.limitations: Partner-evidence/vanity risk: the Positive signal rests on unpublished, unnamed-partner devtrial data.

wideReview.finding: Wide review was requested early and was substantive: TAG design review #1169 (ambivalent, with a detailed Mozilla critique on IETF-vs-W3C venue and on three-party-model/key-binding complexity); Mozilla position Defer (#1316); WebKit No signal (#578), informally preferring to augment WebOTP/OTPs and IMAP clients. The team acted on venue feedback by splitting the spec into an IETF Internet-Draft (protocol) and a W3C/WICG browser API, and starting an IETF dispatch. Two of three other engines are non-supportive at OT start.
wideReview.limitations: No formal WebKit position (issue still open); Mozilla plans to re-open its review; TAG not yet re-reviewed after the spec split.

experiment.finding: Origin Trial is the active phase. Intent to Experiment (2026-05-20) was approved (LGTM M150-M153 by API owner Yoav Weiss, conditional on merging explainers + a debuggability review); OT began ~2026-06-30 in M150 desktop (record shows desktop_first 150 / desktop_last 156). Strong experiment design: five explicit hypotheses, use counter 5916, UMA Blink.Evp.* metrics, third-party support. Genuinely seeded with issuers (Gmail at OT maturity; Hello exploratory) and a second Chromium-based browser OT (Edge). As of cutoff (~3 weeks in) no interim OT readout exists; the public ChromeStatus usage timeline now shows a near-zero measured page-load share (USAGE-1), but no OT-specific adoption/task-completion results.
experiment.limitations: No OT-specific results/adoption/task-completion yet (the cached ChromeStatus telemetry in USAGE-1 is all-Chrome page-load share, not OT adoption); desktop-only; OT registration does not equal production use.

prepareToShip.finding: Not reached. Chrome status is Proposed, is_released=false, there is no Intent to Ship and no stable milestone; the only launch event is the Origin trial. The feature remains in field experiment.
prepareToShip.limitations: Cannot assess ship-readiness; no ship-decision evidence exists.

release.finding: No stable release. However a coordinated public-trial-milestone communication exists: the Chrome for Developers OT blog (2026-07-08) with issuer/verifier demos, requirements, limitations and sign-up; Gmail participation; corroborated by independent press. Messaging accurately frames it as experimental/limited (websites must opt in; other providers must join).
release.limitations: No independent measurement of OT-comms reach or task outcomes; vendor-driven narrative.

adoption.finding: Production adoption remains unassessable, though first-party Chrome usage telemetry is now cached. Use counter 5916 (kEmailVerificationProtocol) is an exact, single-feature mapping (scope=exact, confidence=exact; also registered as the M150 OT-stage counter, but the cached series is the all-Chrome feature-popularity timeline). The cached timeline (22 points) shows the counter fired on a near-zero and declining share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads: first/peak day_percentage 0.000001 on 2026-06-19 (pre-OT; likely Canary/flag/devtrial usage), 0 at the M150 Origin-Trial stable checkpoint (2026-06-30), and latest day_percentage 1e-8 on 2026-07-21. Launch-relative checkpoints for the M150 Origin trial (stable 2026-06-30): atStable day_percentage 0; day30, day90, day180 all null (too early). This page-load share is not production verifier adoption, unique sites, users, task success, or end-user benefit, and no production verifier deployment, framework/library integration, case study, predeclared adoption target, or retention evidence exists, so production adoption cannot be scored.
adoption.limitations: Cached usage telemetry (USAGE-1) reports the share of measured Chrome page loads where the counter fired; counter instrumentation may include detection/probing and reflects the desktop-dominant OT milestone with enterprise/opt-out populations underrepresented. Near-zero page-load share is not zero real-world use and is not production adoption. No unique-site, production-verifier-deployment, retention, task-success, user-benefit, or predeclared-target evidence; cannot score production adoption.

support.finding: Too early to assess. No public support issue taxonomy, known-issues material, or recurrence data exists yet; debuggability is still being developed.

deprecation.finding: New feature in Origin Trial; nothing is deprecated or removed.

developerValue.finding: The developer problem is real, recurring and plausibly valuable (email-verification friction raises customer-acquisition cost; phishing-prone magic links; spam-folder latency), and the website-side change is a one-line progressive enhancement (hidden input with autocomplete=email-verification-token). But demand is unvalidated in production, value is contingent on email-provider participation (chicken-and-egg), and Mozilla questioned the problem-statement detail and trade-offs vs OID/Passkeys. ChromeStatus Web developers: Positive rests on unpublished devtrial data.
developerValue.limitations: No published partner outcomes or measured conversion-cost reduction.

endUserImpact.finding: Hypothesized benefits (no app-switch, no spam-folder wait, cryptographic proof, optional private per-site email addresses) are plausible but unmeasured. No user study, prompt-acceptance, or conversion data. Coverage gap: requires a logged-in webmail session, excluding native-mail-client users (e.g. Thunderbird) flagged internally; the permission-prompt UX is still being designed.
endUserImpact.limitations: No measured end-user impact; too early.

adoption.finding: Unscored: there is no predeclared adoption target and no corroborating production/retention evidence, so the measured level/trend is described rather than verdicted. Issuer-side seeding is real (Gmail at OT maturity; Hello exploratory) and a second Chromium-based browser OT (Edge) is running, but measured Chrome usage is near-zero and declining: exact use counter 5916 (kEmailVerificationProtocol, scope=exact) first/peak day_percentage 0.000001 on 2026-06-19, 0 at the M150 Origin-Trial stable checkpoint (2026-06-30), latest day_percentage 1e-8 on 2026-07-21; atStable 0; day30/day90/day180 null. This page-load share is not unique sites, users, production verifier deployment, task success, or end-user benefit. No production verifier adoption, framework/library integration, case study, retention, or predeclared target. is_official_web_feature=false / web_feature=Missing feature (not yet in web-features/Baseline tracking).
adoption.limitations: USAGE-1 is page-load share where counter 5916 fired, not production adoption; near-zero is not automatically failure and there is no predeclared target to compare against, so the outcome is unscored (not mixed). Counter instrumentation may include detection/probing and reflects the desktop-dominant OT milestone. Edge is Chromium-based, not an independent engine. No unique-site, production, retention, task-success, user-benefit, or target evidence.

interoperability.finding: Process is correct (W3C/WICG browser API + IETF protocol I-D with dispatch started; multiple issuers; Chrome + Edge OTs) but the cross-engine outcome is weak and negative-leaning: Mozilla Defer (substantive explainer critiques), WebKit No signal and informally preferring a WebOTP/OTPs+IMAP alternative, TAG ambivalent, no independent-engine implementation, no web-platform-tests, no Baseline path. The I-D itself flags a live divergence: the W3C browser API uses a now-deprecated request format vs the I-D's HTTP Message Signatures.
interoperability.limitations: OT-stage; interop is not yet expected, but signals are non-supportive, not neutral.

implementationQuality.finding: A real Chromium implementation exists and is shipping in OT with real issuers (Gmail), autofill-based, with origin binding. Known gaps: desktop-only (Android/WebView/iOS not yet); fenced frames and cross-origin documents temporarily disabled (CL 7868831); debuggability still in development; no WPT; a live spec divergence on request format; the ChromeStatus record is still evolving.
implementationQuality.limitations: No regression/bug detail retrieved from crbug; no conformance evidence.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Primary documentation is strong and accurate (detailed explainer, IETF I-D, W3C browser API, devtrial HOWTO, OT blog with demos and stated limitations), corroborated by independent press. The first-party ChromeStatus usage timeline for counter 5916 is now cached and renders a usable near-zero, declining page-load-share series. But the ChromeStatus Web developers: Positive signal rests on an unnamed-partner devtrial; the two explainers diverged at Intent time (flagged by the API owner, later merged); and no public interim OT readout (adoption counts, task completion, partner outcomes, prompt-acceptance) or OT dashboard exists at cutoff.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Vendor-authored primary docs; limited independent validation of claims.

support.finding: No support history yet (OT started ~3 weeks before cutoff). Cannot score.

overall.finding: Overall outcome is not yet scorable. This is a well-motivated, thoughtfully-designed early Origin Trial (real problem, multi-party activation economics, progressive enhancement, active standards engagement, genuine issuer/browser seeding) that is pre-shipment (~3 weeks into a desktop-only OT). First-party Chrome usage telemetry now exists and shows a near-zero, declining measured page-load share (counter 5916; USAGE-1), but with no predeclared target and no production/retention evidence the adoption outcome remains unscored; there is still no measured end-user-impact or support data, no independent-engine support, and non-supportive Mozilla/TAG/WebKit signals with a pending Mozilla re-review. Per the scoring rules, success cannot be inferred from the OT launch, vendor comms, near-zero telemetry, or absent criticism; and most outcome dimensions are unscored.
overall.limitations: Definitive overall scoring requires a post-OT readout, adoption data, and resolved peer-engine positions.

supports: Lifecycle stages (110/120/130/140/150/160); Origin Trial desktop_first 150 desktop_last 156; OT id 10696049115004929; use counter 5916
Browser positions: Mozilla Defer, WebKit No signal, Web developers Positive
Not released; Chrome status Proposed; flag #email-verification-protocol; Blink>Identity; enterprise impact Low
Explainer/spec/intent/devtrial/sample/measurement links
sources.limitations: Cached snapshot; some stage fields null; the webdev Positive position is self-reported.

supports: Early directional TAG review request; multi-stakeholder feedback links; ambivalent assessment per ChromeStatus/intent
sources.limitations: Early review; not re-reviewed after spec split.

supports: Substantive critique: bulk belongs at IETF not W3C; HTML parts to WHATWG; three-party model/key-binding/attestation complexity unjustified; no revocation/transparency; selective-disclosure concerns
sources.limitations: Mozilla individual position, pre-spec-split; partial text retrieved.

supports: Position: Defer; explainer insufficient on problem statement and trade-offs vs OID/Passkeys; filed issue #27; author responded; topic HTML, venue W3C CG
sources.limitations: Filed early for directional guidance; Mozilla plans to re-open after explainer updates.

supports: State open; no formal position (No signal); Chrome OT notice M150 from 2026-06-30; TPAC preference to augment WebOTP/OTPs + IMAP
Initial public proposal; incubation venue; problem framing vs magic-links and social login
Sample/demo and doc; requires Chrome Canary + flag #email-verification-protocol + logged-in email domain; one-line progressive enhancement
sources.limitations: Author blog; single-page demo, not a task-based integration test.

supports: Official OT launch announcement; Gmail participating as provider; issuer/verifier demos; requirements and limitations; M150
Independent confirmation of OT in Chrome 150 and Gmail day-one provider; balanced note that other providers must join and it will not replace codes everywhere
sources.limitations: Secondary press largely summarizing the announcement.

sources.publisher: Chrome Platform Status (ChromeStatus feature-popularity timeline; use-counter bucket 5916 kEmailVerificationProtocol)
supports: Exact, single-feature use-counter mapping: bucket 5916 kEmailVerificationProtocol maps to this ChromeStatus feature (scope=exact, confidence=exact); not a broader WebDX family/property or origin-trial-only counter (also registered as the M150 OT-stage counter, but the cached series is the all-Chrome feature-popularity timeline).

Cached timeline (22 points; SHA256 2295144eaf3aa1fcfd920275514859b2c3452b2c1b62c695f103509df18a73b7) shows the counter fired on a near-zero, declining share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads: first/peak day_percentage 0.000001 on 2026-06-19 (pre-OT; likely Canary/flag/devtrial), 0 at the M150 Origin-Trial stable checkpoint (2026-06-30), and latest day_percentage 1e-8 on 2026-07-21.
Launch-relative checkpoints for the M150 Origin trial (stable 2026-06-30): atStable day_percentage 0; day30, day90, day180 all null (too early).
Interpretation: numerator = Chrome page loads on which counter 5916 fired at least once; denominator = measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads across official channels and platforms.
sources.limitations: Post-cutoff usage-telemetry supplement collected 2026-07-22 (data first observed 2026-06-19 through 2026-07-21), appended to the 2026-07-19-scoped retrospective; used only for the adoption-phase and adoption-outcome findings (and reflected where METRIC-1 was previously cited), never for the intake/review/ship-decision phases. day_percentage is the share of measured Chrome page loads where the counter fired - not unique developers, sites, users, successful tasks, satisfaction, production verifier deployment, or end-user benefit. Counter instrumentation may include detection/probing; reflects participating Chrome clients and the dominant milestone (desktop OT), with enterprise/opt-out populations underrepresented. Near-zero or delayed points are not proof of zero real-world use.

supports: Real Chromium implementation exists; autofill-based field population/form submission; opaque-origin handling
sources.limitations: Only summary-level commit evidence via search; individual commits/regressions not deep-read; CL 7868831 (fenced-frame/cross-origin disable) referenced in the intent thread.

supports: Internet-Draft at IETF (draft-00 2026-06, draft-01 2026-07); Workgroup TBD; IETF dispatch started per the intent thread
sources.limitations: Individual draft, not yet adopted by a WG; status will change.

successes: Clear, quantified, recurring problem framing (95% of top-50 sites use email auth; 73% block account creation on verification) tied to measurable business impact (customer-acquisition cost).

Genuine multi-stakeholder design with explicit activation/incentive economics (browsers -> email providers -> websites -> users) and a progressive-enhancement / graceful-degradation model with no user behavioral change when unsupported.
Acted on TAG venue feedback: split the spec into an IETF protocol Internet-Draft (with dispatch started) and a W3C/WICG browser API, aligning to standards venues.
Seeded the Origin Trial with real issuer participation from day one (Gmail at OT maturity, Hello exploratory) and a second browser OT (Edge), strengthening the activation hypothesis.
Honest alternatives-considered section and candid security/privacy considerations, including acknowledged trade-offs (email-as-global-identifier/spam perpetuation; farmability/PII-friction).
Coordinated, accurate OT communication with working issuer/verifier demos and clearly stated limitations.
failures: ChromeStatus Web developers: Positive rests on an unpublished devtrial with unnamed partners - no cohort, methodology, task-completion, or friction evidence; partner-evidence/vanity risk.

Two of three other engines non-supportive at OT start (Mozilla Defer with substantive explainer critiques; WebKit No signal, informally preferring a WebOTP/OTPs+IMAP alternative) - no cross-engine/Baseline path established.
Substantive TAG (Mozilla/Martin Thomson) critique that the three-party model, key binding and attestations are unjustified complexity, not yet publicly refuted.

Implementation shipped into the OT with open gaps: desktop-only; fenced frames and cross-origin documents temporarily disabled; debuggability still in development; no web-platform-tests; live spec divergence where the browser API uses a request format the I-D marks deprecated.
Coverage gap not yet addressed in-trial: requires a logged-in webmail cookie session, excluding native-mail-client users (e.g. Thunderbird), flagged by Chrome engineers during Intent to Prototype.

No public interim OT readout (adoption counts, task completion, partner outcomes, prompt-acceptance) as of cutoff; the public ChromeStatus usage timeline (USAGE-1) now exists but shows near-zero measured page-load share and is not an OT readout, so adoption/end-user/support outcomes remain unscored.
counterfactuals: If the team had published the devtrial cohort, methodology, denominators and task-completion before the Intent to Experiment, the Web developers: Positive signal would be auditable and Mozilla's insufficient-detail deferral might have been faster to resolve.
If the W3C browser-API and IETF protocol had aligned on the request format before the OT, the deprecated-format divergence flagged in draft-01 would not be shipping in the trial.
If Android and native-mail-client (no-webmail-cookie) coverage had been in OT scope, the coverage gap raised internally (Reilly Grant/Daniel Bratell) would now have real-world test data.

Public ChromeStatus usage telemetry now exists (USAGE-1) but adoption is still unscored: page-load share is not production adoption, and without a predeclared adoption target plus unique-site/production/retention evidence, a counter timeline alone would not have made adoption scorable - a target declared before the OT, with production/retention readouts, would have.
If Mozilla/WebKit had been co-designing (not only giving directional guidance) before the OT, the ambivalent/Defer/No-signal interop picture might differ; the team is now re-opening Mozilla.
skillImprovements: Phase 03 (developer trials): add a rule that a Web developers: Positive ChromeStatus signal may not be cited as sufficient evidence in an Intent to Experiment without published cohort, methodology, denominators and task-completion; treat unnamed-partner claims as partial.

Phase 04 (wide review): add a non-supportive-peer-engine rule - if Mozilla or WebKit is not positive at OT start, interoperability must start at mixed-or-below and the report must surface the specific objections (venue, design complexity) rather than treating No signal as neutral.
Add a split-spec alignment gate: an Origin Trial must not ship a request format the normative spec already marks deprecated; add an automated explainer<->spec divergence check.

Phase 05 (experiment): add an artifact template requiring a public interim readout (unique-site reach, production verifier deployment, retention, task-completion, prompt-acceptance - not just the ChromeStatus counter page-load-share timeline) + measurement dashboard + extension rationale; mark reports partial/blocked if an OT has run >2 weeks with no readout.
Add a coverage/activation check that scores users reachable (e.g. native-mail-client users without webmail cookies) so coverage gaps are visible rather than hidden by desktop first.

Add a telemetry-interpretation rule for adoption scoring: a cached ChromeStatus feature-popularity timeline reports the share of measured Chrome HTTP/HTTPS page loads where a counter fired (only 'exact' when the mapping is exact, not a broader WebDX family/property or origin-trial-only counter); it must not be read as production verifier adoption, unique-site reach, retention, task success, or end-user benefit, and near-zero values must not be scored as failure where there is no predeclared target - the adoption outcome stays unscored and describes the measured level/trend instead.
Add a regression eval including an early-OT case (mostly unscored outcomes) to train the model not to infer success from vendor blog claims, press sentiment, or near-zero telemetry.

missingEvidence: No Origin Trial readout (adoption counts, task completion, partner outcomes, prompt-acceptance) - OT began ~2026-06-30, only ~3 weeks before cutoff; no interim/final readout published.
No measurable end-user impact (no user study, conversion data, prompt-UX acceptance data).

ChromeStatus usage telemetry for counter 5916 is now cached (USAGE-1) and shows near-zero measured page-load share, but no unique-site, production-verifier-deployment, retention, task-success, user-benefit, or predeclared-adoption-target evidence exists (page-load share is not production adoption or a target).
No support/issue taxonomy or known-issues history (too early).
No framework/library/tool integration or production verifier case studies (too early).
No independent-engine implementation or Baseline/web-features progress; Mozilla and WebKit positions unresolved.
Did not retrieve full IETF dispatch discussion or individual Chromium commits/crbug regressions.
